did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically two convolutional neural networks (a simple CNN with six 2D convolutional layers and a custom VGG-16 model with 3D convolutional layers), to classify skin lesions (nevi, melanomas, and basal cell carcinomas) using multispectral images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors trained two specific convolutional neural networks (a simple CNN and a custom VGG-16) on a proprietary dataset of 509 multispectral images (327 nevi, 112 melanomas, 70 BCCs) to classify skin lesions. It reports new results, including specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 83% for the simple CNN with full spectral cubes), and discusses the performance of their own models rather than just summarizing prior literature.

title: Training State-of-the-Art Deep Learning Algorithms with Visible and Extended Near-Infrared Multispectral Images of Skin Lesions for the Improvement of Skin Cancer Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study evaluated the use of visible and extended near-infrared multispectral imaging to improve deep learning-based classification of skin lesions, specifically nevi, melanomas, and basal cell carcinomas. The researchers trained two convolutional neural networks—a simple CNN with 2D layers and a custom VGG-16 with 3D layers—on a proprietary dataset of 509 multispectral images. The main evaluation approach compared model performance using full spectral cubes versus RGB-simulated images derived from the three closest spectral bands. The simple CNN achieved the highest accuracy of 83% with full spectral cubes, while the custom VGG-16 reached 71%, both significantly outperforming their RGB-simulated counterparts. These results demonstrate that multispectral imaging provides complementary information beyond traditional RGB images, enhancing classification performance for non-invasive skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class; 112 samples in the proprietary dataset (including 1 MIS case); used as a primary target for classification against benign lesions and other malignancies.
Benign class; 327 samples in the proprietary dataset; grouped as 'benign' for binary classification tasks.
Malignant class; 70 samples in the proprietary dataset; used as a target for classification.
Malignant class; 1 sample in the proprietary dataset; included in the dataset but the primary focus of the study was on the three main classes (Nevi, Melanoma, BCC).

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Nevi, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma) and binary malignant vs. benign status.
all_task_types.details: The study performs multiclass classification of skin lesions into three specific diagnostic categories (327 nevi, 112 melanomas, 70 BCCs) using a proprietary dataset of multispectral images. It also evaluates binary classification performance (Malignant vs. Benign). The models (simple CNN and custom VGG-16) output class predictions evaluated via accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: Proprietary Multispectral Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of multispectral (MS) images collected using a custom LED-based MS imaging system (VIS-NIR range, 8 spectral bands: 414, 447, 477, 524, 671, 735, 890, and 995 nm). Data was acquired from patients at the Melanoma Unit of the Dermatology Department at Hospital Clinic i Provincial de Barcelona (Spain) and the Skin Cancer Unit at Policlinico di Modena-University of Modena and Reggio Emilia (Italy). The dataset consists of spectral cubes derived from reflectance images. The final selected subset for the study included 509 lesions chosen to balance the classes of nevi, melanomas, and basal cell carcinomas.

use.training: Used to train two convolutional neural networks: a simple CNN with six 2D convolutional layers and a custom VGG-16 model with 3D convolutional layers. The models were trained using full spectral cubes and compared against RGB-simulated images.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions using cross-validation with optimal partitions to improve model performance in the conclusion, but specific validation counts or splits for the primary experiment are not detailed in the provided text.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper reports final accuracy metrics (83% for simple CNN, 71% for VGG-16) on the dataset, implying a hold-out or cross-validated test set, but specific test counts are not provided.
use.other: The dataset was also used to generate RGB-simulated images by selecting the three spectral bands closest to RGB wavelengths for comparative analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train models and evaluate performance by splitting data into folds, ensuring robust estimation of model accuracy and consistency across different training instances.
To derive classification metrics including Accuracy, Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Precision (P), and F1 score for each lesion class and for the binary malignant vs. benign task.
To measure the proportion of correct predictions over the unseen test set, averaged among lesion classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between multispectral and RGB-simulated inputs were based on accuracy metrics (e.g., 83% vs 36% for the simple CNN) without statistical significance testing.

Proprietary Multispectral Skin Lesion Da.description: Dataset of 509 multispectral images (327 nevi, 112 melanomas, 70 BCCs) with 8 spectral bands (414, 447, 477, 524, 671, 735, 890, 995 nm). Evaluated on an unseen test set comprising 20% of the data.

experiments.name: RGB-Simulated Image Training
experiments.input_data: Three spectral bands closest to RGB wavelengths (447, 524, and 671 nm)

Proprietary Multispectral Skin Lesion Da.notes: The simple custom 2D-CNN achieved the highest accuracy (83%) with full spectral cubes. The custom 3D VGG-16 achieved 71% accuracy with full spectral cubes. Both models significantly outperformed their RGB-simulated counterparts (36% and 45% respectively). The 3D VGG-16 model was noted as retrieving the most consistent outcomes. Saliency maps indicated model attention focused on the lesion area rather than surrounding skin.

architecture_used.main_model: Two models: a simple custom 2D-CNN and a custom VGG-16 with 3D convolutional layers.
architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; models were trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 128x128 pixels with 8 spectral bands (reflectance cube shape: 128, 128, 8). For RGB-simulated experiments, input was 3 spectral bands (447, 524, 671 nm).

simple_2d_cnn.layers: Six 2D convolutional layers followed by a final dense layer.
simple_2d_cnn.stride: Stride 1 in odd layers, stride 2 in even layers.
simple_2d_cnn.activation: ReLU for convolutional layers; Softmax for the final dense layer.

custom_3d_vgg16.layers: Thirteen 3D convolutional layers, five 3D max pooling layers, and three dense layers.
custom_3d_vgg16.activation: ReLU for convolutional layers; Softmax for the output layer.
custom_3d_vgg16.regularization: Dropout regularization with a value of 0.5 in fully connected layers.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (TensorFlow/Keras default or unspecified).
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (likely categorical cross-entropy for classification).

hyperparameters.batch_size: Varied from 2 to 10 via random grid search.
hyperparameters.learning_rate: Varied from 10^-5 to 10^-6 via random grid search.

training_details.training_schedule: Random grid search strategy for hyperparameters. Balanced set used. Training performed on 80% of data with cross-validation; 20% held out as unseen test set.
training_details.hardware: AMD Ryzen 9 3950X processor, 64GB RAM, Quadro RTX 4000 GPU. Software: Python 3.8.12, TensorFlow GPU 2.4, Keras 2.4.3, CUDA v. 10.8.

classification_or_output_head.classes: 3 classes (Nevi, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma) and binary classification (Malignant vs. Benign).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Simple CNN (2D convolutional neural network with six 2D convolutional layers)
preprocessing_techniques: Reflectance normalization/calibration using a neutral gray sample (X-Rite ColorChecker) and dark images to compensate for straylight noise, calculating reflectance values RλLesion(i,j) = k * (SλLesion(i,j) - SλDark(i,j)) / (SλNeutral(i,j) - SλDark(i,j)).
Image resizing to 128x128 pixels for neural network input; larger resolutions were tested but discarded due to memory errors without accuracy improvement.
Visual inspection to discard images with artifacts or hair before processing.
Selection of three specific spectral bands (447, 524, and 671 nm) closest to RGB wavelengths to create RGB-simulated images from the full spectral cube.
Data augmentation including rotations and random flips applied to the training set.
Use of Keras experimental preprocessing layers to construct an augmentation pipeline for synchronous GPU training.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set as part of a robust data augmentation scheme to handle the small balanced training dataset (210 images). Implemented using Keras' experimental preprocessing layers for synchronous GPU training. The total number of reflectance cubes was augmented by a factor of 50.

Used to address class imbalance in the unbalanced dataset. A custom function based on Python’s compute_class_weight was used to generate class weights for the loss function (categorical cross-entropy) during training.

Used to equalize numbers across each class by creating a balanced subset of the dataset (210 images total) to improve discrimination of malignant lesions, yielding similar accuracy results to using the full unbalanced dataset with class weighting.
Tested but yielded poor outcomes; therefore, it was not used in the final model training.
Tested; did not produce negative results, but the authors opted for caution with this type of augmentation.

conclusions: Multispectral (MS) imaging provides complementary information beyond traditional RGB images, significantly enhancing the classification performance of deep learning models for skin lesion diagnosis.
The simple custom 2D-CNN achieved the highest classification accuracy of 83% when trained with full spectral cubes, compared to 36% with RGB-simulated images.
The custom VGG-16 model achieved an accuracy of 71% with full spectral cubes and 45% with RGB-simulated images, demonstrating consistent outcomes across different training instances.

Using full spectral cubes (8 bands: 414, 447, 477, 524, 671, 735, 890, and 995 nm) is superior to using only three spectral bands closest to RGB wavelengths for classifying nevi, melanomas, and basal cell carcinomas.

The study acknowledges that the small dataset size (509 lesions) is a limitation, but indicates that MS imaging has significant potential for improving non-invasive skin cancer diagnosis as larger datasets become available.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, proprietary dataset of only 509 multispectral images, which the authors explicitly acknowledge as a limitation affecting generalizability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (327 nevi vs. 112 melanomas and 70 BCCs), necessitating the use of undersampling to create a balanced subset (70 per class) for training, which may introduce selection bias.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal proprietary dataset split into 80% training and 20% testing.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between multispectral and RGB-simulated inputs, relying instead on accuracy metrics.

The custom 3D VGG-16 model had a very high parameter count (80,328,515) compared to the simple 2D-CNN (43,123), yet performed worse (71% vs. 83% accuracy), suggesting potential overfitting or inefficiency for this specific small dataset.
Data leakage risks are mitigated by the use of cross-validation and a held-out test set, but the small sample size limits the robustness of these estimates.
Clinical relevance is limited by the lack of integration with standard clinical workflows; the study focuses on algorithmic performance rather than clinical utility or deployment feasibility.
The study acknowledges that achieving 100% accuracy is crucial for healthcare integration, which current DL algorithms have not yet achieved, limiting immediate clinical adoption.
